section.title: The Second Test
section.sectionRationale: The heavenly council convenes a second time, because the first test, though devastating, did not touch Job's own flesh. Satan raises the stakes with a proverb, skin for skin, and God permits an assault on Job's body itself. The chapter ends with Job's wife's despair, Job's second refusal to sin, and the arrival of three friends whose silent sitting is the last unbroken kindness of the book before the speeches begin.
section.thesis: Even when Satan is allowed to strike Job's own body, Job still refuses to curse God, showing his integrity was never dependent on health any more than it was on wealth; his friends' seven days of silence is real comfort, soon to be squandered.
section.frameNote: Four scenes: the second council and the boil-covered test; Job's wife urging him to curse God and die; Job's reply that both good and adversity come from God's hand; and the three friends arriving to weep, tear their robes, and sit with him in silence.

units.label: The second council
units.note: you incited Me against him without cause

verses.text: On another day the sons of God came to present themselves before the LORD, and Satan also came with them to present himself before Him.

"Where have you come from?" said the LORD to Satan. 
"From roaming through the earth," he replied, "and walking back and forth in it."

Then the LORD said to Satan, "Have you considered My servant Job? For there is no one on earth like him, a man who is blameless and upright, who fears God and shuns evil. He still retains his {{tummah|integrity}}, even though you incited Me against him to ruin him {{chinnam|without cause}}."
"Skin for skin!" Satan replied. "A man will give up all he owns in exchange for his life.
But stretch out Your hand and strike his flesh and bones, and he will surely curse You to Your face."
"Very well," said the LORD to Satan. "He is in your hands, but you must spare his life."

units.label: Satan strikes Job's body
units.note: terrible boils from head to foot

verses.text: So Satan went out from the presence of the LORD and infected Job with terrible {{shechin|boils}} from the soles of his feet to the crown of his head.
And Job took a piece of broken pottery to scrape himself as he sat among the ashes.

units.label: Job's wife and Job's reply
units.note: should we accept only good from God

verses.text: Then Job's wife said to him, "Do you still retain your {{tummah|integrity}}? Curse God and die!"

"You speak as a {{nabal|foolish}} woman speaks," he told her. "Should we accept from God only good and not adversity?" 
In all this, Job did not sin in what he said.

units.label: The friends arrive
units.note: seven days and seven nights of silence

verses.text: Now when Job's three friends, Eliphaz the Temanite, Bildad the Shuhite, and Zophar the Naamathite, heard about all this adversity that had come upon him, each of them came from his home, and they met together to go and sympathize with Job and comfort him.
When they lifted up their eyes from afar, they could barely recognize Job. They began to weep aloud, and each man tore his robe and threw dust in the air over his head.
Then they sat on the ground with him for seven days and seven nights, but no one spoke a word to him because they saw how intense his suffering was.

chinnam.gloss: Without cause, for nothing, gratuitously. God Himself uses this word to describe what was done to Job: real, undeserved suffering, inflicted for no fault of his own. This is one of the most theologically loaded phrases in the book, ruling out any simple explanation that Job's suffering was earned.

tummah.gloss: Integrity, blamelessness, the noun form of the same root behind tam in chapter 1. God affirms that Job still holds it after the first test; Job's wife throws the word back at him bitterly, as if integrity were now worthless.

shechin.gloss: A boil or inflamed sore, the same word used for the sixth plague on Egypt. Covering Job from head to foot, it makes him unrecognizable to his own friends and reduces him to sitting among the ashes, scraping himself with broken pottery.

nabal.gloss: A fool, not merely unintelligent but morally and spiritually senseless, one who acts as though God does not matter, as in Psalm 14:1. Job's rebuke of his wife is sharp but aimed at her counsel, not a final verdict on her character; she too has lost everything.

basic.context: The heavenly council meets again. God commends Job's continued integrity, and Satan raises the stakes with a proverb, skin for skin, arguing that Job has only been tested at a comfortable distance. God permits an attack on Job's own body, and Job is covered in boils from head to foot, sitting in ashes and scraping himself with broken pottery. His wife urges him to curse God and die; Job refuses, insisting that a life that receives good from God's hand must also be willing to receive adversity. Three friends arrive, and their first act is real kindness: they weep, they grieve visibly, and then they simply sit with him in silence for seven days.
basic.hermeneutics: God's own words are essential here: He tells Satan that Job was ruined chinnam, without cause. This closes off, before the friends ever speak, any theory that Job's suffering can be explained by some hidden sin. The chapter also shows that grief and lament are not sin; Job's silence-breaking will come next chapter, but here his restraint under unbearable pain is itself part of the book's honest witness to what faithful suffering can look like.
basic.originalLanguages: God twice affirms Job's tummah, integrity, and adds the weighty word chinnam, without cause, ruling out any notion that Job deserved this. The shechin, boils, that cover him head to foot are the same disease named among Egypt's plagues. And Job calls his wife's counsel nabal, the word for the fool who lives as if God does not exist, even while acknowledging her real suffering too.
basic.typology: The proverb 'skin for skin' anticipates a deeper truth still to come: that it will finally cost far more than skin, an entire body given over to suffering, to answer the accuser once and for all. Job's refusal to curse God even in bodily agony foreshadows a greater patience under a far worse affliction.
basic.christ: Job's body is given over to the affliction while his life is spared; Christ's body was given over to affliction and death itself, the ultimate answer to the accuser's slander that no one serves God for God's own sake. Job's silent endurance under the friends' watching eyes, before he ever speaks, faintly anticipates the silence of the suffering servant who, like a lamb before its shearers, did not open his mouth.
basic.god: God permits the escalation but still holds a boundary, Job's life must be spared. He is not indifferent to the extremity of the test, yet He also does not explain Himself to Job in this chapter or protect him from it. The tension between God's sovereignty and Job's suffering is left standing, not resolved.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 53:7 pictures the suffering servant silent before his shearers; 1 Corinthians 10:13 promises a way of escape so believers are not tested beyond what they can bear; James 5:11 points back to Job's endurance as proof that the Lord is full of compassion and mercy in the end.
basic.oneStory: The second test strips away the last possible excuse, Job's health, and still he does not curse God. His friends' seven days of silent grief are the last moment in the book where comfort is offered rightly, before human wisdom opens its mouth and starts getting it wrong.

context.literary: This chapter closes the prose frame that opened the book and sets the stage for the poetic dialogue. Its structure mirrors chapter 1 closely, the same council, the same divine commendation, the same permission and boundary, intensifying the pattern before breaking into a new register with Job's wife's words and the friends' arrival.
context.historical: Boils and ash-heaps evoke the language of covenant curses and plague elsewhere in the Old Testament, yet Job is not under Israel's covenant; his suffering, and his friends' visible grieving customs, torn robes, dust on the head, reflect a broader ancient Near Eastern mourning practice.
context.canonical: James 5:11 will later cite Job's endurance as a model for believers awaiting the Lord's compassionate outcome, showing that the New Testament reads this suffering as ultimately purposeful even though the purpose is hidden from Job himself throughout the book.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To intensify the test to Job's own body while reaffirming, in God's own words, that Job's affliction is chinnam, without cause, so the reader cannot mistake what follows for deserved punishment.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes Job's restraint and his wife's counsel; it does not prescribe harsh rebukes of grieving spouses as a model, nor does it say all suffering sufferers must respond exactly as Job did. The enduring truth is that a life devoted to God receives both good and adversity from His hand without that devotion becoming void.

debates.question: Was Job's rebuke of his wife too harsh, given that she too had lost everything?

views.label: A necessary refusal of counsel that would have led to sin
views.case: Her counsel, curse God and die, was genuinely dangerous, and Job's answer defends the legitimacy of God's right to give and to take away.

views.label: A sharp word toward a woman suffering the same losses he was
views.case: She had buried the same ten children and lost the same fortune; calling her foolish, even if theologically correct, does not acknowledge her own grief, which the text leaves unaddressed.

debates.bearing: The tension is worth naming honestly rather than resolving too quickly; Job's theology is right, but the text does not tell us whether his tone toward his suffering wife was entirely without harshness.

debates.question: What does 'skin for skin' mean in Satan's proverb?

views.label: People will trade any possession, even another person's skin, to save their own
views.case: Satan argues self-preservation is the deepest human motive, implying Job's endurance so far has cost him nothing personal.

views.label: A general proverb about equivalent exchange applied cynically to Job
views.case: The phrase may simply mean like for like, and Satan is applying a common saying to claim Job has not yet been tested where it truly counts.

debates.bearing: Either way, Satan's point is the same: he believes bodily affliction will finally break what wealth and family loss could not.

study.originalLanguages: God repeats that Job holds tummah, integrity, and adds the crucial word chinnam, without cause, when describing what was done to him, closing off any theory of deserved suffering. The shechin, boils, that cover Job recall Egypt's plagues, and Job names his wife's counsel nabal, the fool's rejection of God's ways, even as he continues to bear his own affliction without sinning in what he said.

typology.type: Skin for skin, the escalation from possessions to the body itself
typology.fulfillment: Christ's body, not merely His possessions or family, given over fully in suffering to answer the accuser's slander once for all.

typology.type: Job's silence under affliction before he ever speaks in chapter 3
typology.fulfillment: Isaiah 53:7, the suffering servant led like a lamb to the slaughter, silent before his shearers.

study.christ: Satan escalates from Job's possessions to his very body, arguing that self-preservation will finally expose Job's faith as hollow. It does not. Christ answers this accusation completely: He gave up not merely comfort but His body to death, and did not open His mouth in self-defense, the greater fulfillment of a patience Job only begins to model here. Job's refusal to curse God even in bodily agony is a real but partial foreshadowing of the perfect obedience of the cross.
study.god: God sets the terms of the test again, permitting greater suffering while still drawing a line, Job's life must be spared. He does not explain Himself to Job, and the tension between His sovereignty and Job's unexplained pain is left standing through this chapter, not resolved until the whirlwind speeches near the book's end.

crossReferences.note: The suffering servant, silent before his shearers, anticipated in Job's restraint under bodily affliction.
God will not let His people be tested beyond what they can bear, always providing a way through, echoed in God's boundary on Satan's test.
Points back to Job's endurance as proof that the Lord is compassionate and merciful in the end, even though the book itself withholds that end for many chapters.

study.oneStory: The accuser tries once more to prove Job's faith is only self-interest, and once more Job's integrity holds, though at far greater cost. His friends' first act, silent grief for seven days, is the last unbroken comfort in the book before human wisdom opens its mouth and begins to fail him, setting up everything that follows.

sermon.title: Holding on to Your Faith in the Midst of Suffering: Job, Part 2
sermon.why: Piper walks directly through Job 2:6-10, showing that though Satan struck Job with sores, the text's own commentary traces the ultimate cause to God's sovereign hand, and that Job's reply, shall we receive good from God and not adversity, is the theological anchor for the whole chapter.

quiz.q: What decisive word does God use to describe Job's suffering when speaking to Satan?

options.text: Chinnam, without cause, ruling out the idea that Job's suffering was deserved.
options.note: God's own description in verse 3 closes off any theory of merited punishment before the friends ever speak.

options.text: That Satan incited Him to harm Job, though God still calls it undeserved.
options.note: True to the verse's wording, but less precise than naming the key word chinnam itself as the decisive term.

options.text: That the suffering was Satan's idea entirely, with no divine involvement.
options.note: Satan incites the test, but God permits and bounds it; God's sovereignty over the event is affirmed even as He names it undeserved.

options.text: That Job deserved some measure of correction.
options.note: God explicitly says the opposite, that Job was ruined without cause.

quiz.q: What is the meaning of Satan's proverb, 'skin for skin'?

options.text: That people will give up anything, even someone else's skin, to preserve their own life, implying Job has not yet truly been tested.
options.note: Satan argues that until Job's own body suffers, his devotion remains unproven.

options.text: That self-preservation is the deepest human motive, so only an attack on Job's own body will reveal the truth.
options.note: Captures Satan's underlying logic, though less precise than the exact wording of the proverb about trading skin for skin.

options.text: That Job would gladly trade his life for his children's lives.
options.note: This captures a partial idea of exchange, but misses Satan's specific point about self-preservation as the deepest human motive.

options.text: That Job's skin disease is a punishment for boasting.
options.note: The proverb is about self-preservation, not a specific judgment for boasting.

quiz.q: How does Job answer his wife's counsel to curse God and die?

options.text: By asking whether we should accept only good from God and not adversity, and the narrator affirms he did not sin in what he said.
options.note: Verse 10 gives Job's reply and the narrator's own verdict on it.

options.text: By rebuking her counsel as foolish and insisting we must accept adversity from God as well as good.
options.note: Correct in substance, though less precise than the narrator's own explicit verdict that Job did not sin in what he said.

options.text: By remaining completely silent and not responding at all.
options.note: Job gives a clear verbal answer, recorded in verse 10, rather than silence.

options.text: By cursing God as she suggested, but only privately.
options.note: The text explicitly says Job did not sin in what he said.

quiz.q: What do Job's three friends do first, before any speeches begin?

options.text: They weep aloud, tear their robes, throw dust on their heads, and then sit with him in silence for seven days and nights.
options.note: Verses 12 to 13 describe this as an act of real, costly sympathy before any words of counsel are offered.

options.text: They approach him, grieve visibly, and stay with him for an extended time before saying anything.
options.note: True in substance, though less specific than the exact seven days and nights of silence the text describes.

options.text: They immediately begin explaining why Job must have sinned.
options.note: That accusing counsel comes only later, starting in chapter 4; here they simply grieve and sit in silence.

options.text: They refuse to approach him because of his condition.
options.note: They come close enough to weep and sit with him, despite barely recognizing him.

quiz.q: Why is it significant that God twice affirms Job still holds his tummah, his integrity?

options.text: It shows Job's uprightness has survived both the loss of wealth and family and the loss of health, proving it was never dependent on comfort.
options.note: God's repeated affirmation in 2:3 tracks the escalation of the test and confirms Job's devotion is not transactional, directly answering Satan's original accusation in chapter 1.

options.text: It shows that God's earlier assessment of Job still stands even after far greater loss.
options.note: True as far as it goes, but less specific than naming exactly what has escalated, the loss of health after the loss of wealth and family.

options.text: It shows that Job never experienced any real doubt or struggle.
options.note: The coming chapters will show Job in deep anguish and doubt; integrity does not mean the absence of struggle.

options.text: It proves Job earned back God's favor through good behavior.
options.note: God's commendation precedes and does not depend on any new deeds; it is the same verdict repeated, not favor Job earns anew.

questions: Job says we must accept adversity from God's hand as readily as good. Where in your own life do you find that harder to actually live out than to say?

Job's friends' first and best gift was simply sitting with him in silence for seven days. Who in your life is suffering right now, and what would it cost you to sit with them before you try to explain anything?
Job's wife had lost the same children and the same fortune, yet the text leaves her grief largely unaddressed. How do you make room for the grief of others even when their words in pain trouble you?

God calls Job's suffering 'without cause,' closing the door on simple explanations. Where are you tempted to manufacture a reason for suffering, yours or someone else's, rather than sit with the fact that some suffering has no tidy explanation?
Job's integrity held through the loss of health as well as wealth. What would have to be stripped from your life before you discovered what your own faith is really resting on?

translationNotes.note: Without cause renders chinnam, a strong term meaning gratuitously or for no reason, used elsewhere for gifts given freely; here it underscores that Job's suffering was in no way earned.
Curse God and die uses the same euphemistic use of barak, bless, for curse found in chapter 1, softening the plain sense of the words at the point of greatest danger to God's honor in the text.

The BSB names all three friends together in one sentence; the underlying Hebrew lists their names and hometowns as a formal, almost legal introduction, marking their arrival as a significant event in the narrative.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Job 2:1-13. The em dashes around the friends' names in verse 11 in the source text were replaced with commas per house style; no other substantive changes were made to the verse text.
